coredata.dc:title: Engineering of brewery waste-derived graphene quantum dots with ZnO nanoparticles for treating multi-drug resistant bacterial infections

coredata.dc:description: In this study, the antibacterial efficiency of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) derived from brewery spent grain (BSG) in combination with zinc oxide (ZnO) nanoparticles against the methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA) isolated from clinical wound specimens is investigated for the first time. The crystallinity, morphology, and structural defects of the nanomaterials were analysed in detail by the X-ray diffraction (XRD), field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM), and Raman spectroscopy techniques. The layered morphology of BSG-derived reduced graphene oxide (BrGO), d-spacing, and the average particle size of GQDs were further examined by a high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM). Compared to individual GQDs and ZnO, ZnO/GQDs composites showed a better antibacterial activity against Escherichia coli, and Staphylococcus aureus. ZnO/GQDs also demonstrated significant efficiency in disinfecting the MRSA (ATCC and clinical isolates from wound specimens). Density functional theory (DFT) calculations confirmed the substantial clustering of functional groups at the edges of nanomaterial. The disinfection mechanism of MRSA is elucidated for the first time with DFT calculations. Cytotoxicity experiments with the 3T3 mouse embryo fibroblasts testified that ZnO/GQDs are not toxic to mammalian cells.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was conducted as a part of the International Research Agendas PLUS programme of the Foundation for Polish Science, co-financed by the European Union under the European Regional Development Fund (MAB PLUS/2019/11). All computations were carried out using computational resources of the ‘Blue-ocean cluster’, Lodz University of Technology. We would like to acknowledge Dr. Anna Jędrzejczak from the Institute of Materials Science and Engineering, Lodz University of Technology for providing support in the Raman analysis for the revised manuscript. Also, we would like to acknowledge Dr. Slawomir Kadlubowski, Interministerial Institute of Radiation Technology, Lodz University of Technology for the Zetapotential analysis. One of the authors SS would like to acknowledge the Department of Microbiology, Sidra Medicine for antimicrobial studies. The supporting information contains XRD patterns of BrGO, GQDs, and ZnO/GQDs (Figure S1), EDS mapping of BrGO, and element distribution on the surface of BrGO (Figure S2 (a) and (b)), a comparison of Raman spectra at different excitation wavelengths (Figure S3), PL spectra (Figure S4), AFM results of ZnO and ZnO/GQDs (Figure S5), a comparison of FTIR spectra measured for brewery GQD flakes, and theoretical predictions for graphene and w4 structures optimized in PCM water environment (Figure S6), comparison of Raman spectra measured for brewery GQD flakes and theoretical predictions for graphene and w4 structures optimized in PCM water environment (Figure S7). The supporting information also contains a table of comparison of key findings of studies involved with ZnO/GQDs nanocomposites for antibacterial applications (Table S1) and RMS values of the comparison of optimized structure w1 at different chosen theory levels related to the initial geometry (Table S2).

This research was conducted as a part of the International Research Agendas PLUS programme of the Foundation for Polish Science, co-financed by the European Union under the European Regional Development Fund (MAB PLUS/2019/11). All computations were carried out using computational resources of the ‘Blue-ocean cluster’, Lodz University of Technology. We would like to acknowledge Dr. Anna Jędrzejczak from the Institute of Materials Science and Engineering, Lodz University of Technology for providing support in the Raman analysis for the revised manuscript. Also, we would like to acknowledge Dr. Slawomir Kadlubowski, Interministerial Institute of Radiation Technology, Lodz University of Technology for the Zetapotential analysis. One of the authors SS would like to acknowledge the Department of Microbiology, Sidra Medicine for antimicrobial studies.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FT-IR spectra of BrGO, ZnO, GQDs, and ZnO/GQDs. Every spectrum offers a significant understanding of the molecular variations and chemical properties of the corresponding nanomaterial.

FE-SEM images of (a) and (b) BrGO, (c) and (d) ZnO nanoparticles. The pictures present the surface characteristics and nanostructures, providing important insights into the morphology of the nanomaterials.

Raman spectra of (a) BrGO, (b) ZnO, (c) ZnO/GQDs nanocomposites. Spectrum integration between 1580 and 2700 cm−1 on the Raman map of (d) ZnO (e) BrGO, (f) and (g) GQDs/ZnO nanocomposite which is responsible for the E2 g phonon in graphitic material.

(a) XPS survey spectra of BrGO, ZnO, and ZnO/GQDs nanocomposites, (b) The C1s spectra of BrGO, (c) P2p spectra of BrGO, (d) O1s spectra of BrGO, (e) C1s spectra of ZnO, (f) O1s spectra of ZnO, (g) Zn 2p spectra of ZnO, (h) C1s spectra of ZnO/GQDs, i) Zn 2p spectra of ZnO/GQDs.

TEM images of (a) BrGO, (b) GQDs, (d) and (e) ZnO nanoparticles, (c) and (f) size distribution histograms of GQDs and ZnO nanoparticles. This demonstrates the size, shape, and distribution of ZnO, GQDs, and BrGO, as well as provides information about their layering and morphology.
Zeta potential analysis. A bar chart showing the zeta potential of ZnO, GQDs and ZnO/GQDs. Results are presented as means ± SD.

Schematic of the mechanism of BrGO and GQDs formation. The mechanism controlling the conversion of BSG into GQDs is visually represented by the diagram, which highlights the important processes and interactions involved in the synthesis.

Molecular structure and electron density ESP of computed GQD structures. The molecular structures shed light on how the atoms are arranged within the GQDs, and the ESP maps present a thorough picture of the charge density and electron distribution throughout the quantum dots.
MEF viability after 24-h treatment of rGO, ZnO, GQDs, and ZnO/GQDs. Yellow, orange, purple, and violet bars represent BrGO, ZnO, GQDs, and ZnO/GQDs composites respectively.
Minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) of ZnO, BrGO, GQDs, and ZnO/GQDs for (a) E. coli and (b) S. aureus.

Antimicrobial experiments: photographs of (a) BrGO, (b) ZnO, and (c) ZnO/GQDs for ATCC MRSA strain (d) photograph of ZnO/GQDs for MRSA clinical isolates from wound specimen. The zones of inhibition are indicated by the yellow dotted circles. Viable count of bacteria after exposure to (e) ZnO, and (f) ZnO/GQDs composite for clinical MRSA. All values represent the mean ± SD ** p < 0.05.

Schematic representation of MRSA outer membrane structure with its key peptidoglycan chains cross-linked with peptide stems compared with all-atom structure representation and the bacterial disruption mechanism using ZnO/GQDs.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, the antibacterial efficiency of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) derived from brewery spent grain (BSG) in combination with zinc oxide (ZnO) nanoparticles against the methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA) isolated from clinical wound specimens is investigated for the first time. The crystallinity, morphology, and structural defects of the nanomaterials were analysed in detail by the X-ray diffraction (XRD), field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM), and Raman spectroscopy techniques. The layered morphology of BSG-derived reduced graphene oxide (BrGO), d-spacing, and the average particle size of GQDs were further examined by a high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM). Compared to individual GQDs and ZnO, ZnO/GQDs composites showed a better antibacterial activity against Escherichia coli, and Staphylococcus aureus. ZnO/GQDs also demonstrated significant efficiency in disinfecting the MRSA (ATCC and clinical isolates from wound specimens). Density functional theory (DFT) calculations confirmed the substantial clustering of functional groups at the edges of nanomaterial. The disinfection mechanism of MRSA is elucidated for the first time with DFT calculations. Cytotoxicity experiments with the 3T3 mouse embryo fibroblasts testified that ZnO/GQDs are not toxic to mammalian cells.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Brewery waste-derived graphene quantum dots with zinc oxide nanoparticles to disinfect superbugs

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: For over 70 years, antibiotics have been utilized for treating illnesses caused by bacteria and other microbes [1]. Antibiotics have undoubtedly benefited mankind by protecting countless lives in the fight against microorganisms [2]. Nevertheless, multi-drug resistant (MDR) bacterial infections are becoming more prevalent all over the world [3]. The growing proliferation of MDR microbes poses a significant healthcare concern, which necessitates the invention of potential antimicrobial materials and therapies [4]. In response to this, nanomaterials have been widely investigated during the last few decades for the development of new, potent antibacterial agents against MDR infections [5] , [6].

Owing to its special qualities including easy dispersion, biocompatibility, strong electrical conductivity, and low cytotoxicity, graphene-based materials are attracting much attention nowadays [8] , [7]. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are zero-dimensional nanomaterials that have transformed into multifaceted quasi-spherical substances having a strong boundary impact and photostability [9,10]. GQDs are typically less than 10 nm in size [11], making them much smaller than conventional graphene sheets and the edges of the GQDs can be either zigzag or armchair configurations [10]. These structural configurations facilitate the adherence of GQDs to the biomolecular matrix of microorganisms [12]. Top-down and bottom-up procedures can be commonly used to synthesis GQDs [13]. Top-down technique involves exfoliating and cutting graphite or single-walled carbon nanotubes. The bottom-up synthesis of GQDs is slightly difficult because it involves complex chemical reactions and relies on specific chemicals [14].

There is an urgent need to systematically analyse the antibacterial properties of various graphene derivatives (graphene oxide (GO), GQDs, and carbon QDs). The antimicrobial efficiency is influenced by the diameter size, lateral size, and chemical composition of graphene derivatives [15]. Even though, photo-induced production of singlet oxygen occurs in all types of QDs, nitrogen-doped carbon QDs were effective to disinfect a wide range of microbes (Enterobacter aerogenes, Proteus mirabilis, Staphylococcus saprophyticus, Listeria monocytogenes, Salmonella typhimurium, and Klebsiella pneumoniae) [15] .

GQDs can generate a significant number of free electrons and holes because of their strong electron transport mechanism [16]. The modification of electron and quantum confinement behavior of GQDs has become interesting, particularly in contrast to graphene [17]. This improves the antibacterial capabilities of GQDs via mechanisms such as peroxidase or oxidase-like activities [18]. GQDs exhibit a high amount of sp [2] hybridization in their structure, which contributes to their outstanding electrical, optical, and mechanical properties. The bacterial inactivation by GQDs can also occur without light irradiation owing to its unique structural features especially the presence of electron-transport species in highly oxidative graphitic structure [19].

Zinc oxide (ZnO) nanoparticles have been commercially used for the disinfection of various microbes because of the generation of reactive oxygen species by ZnO under UV or simulated solar light irradiation [20,21]. The antibacterial properties of ZnO nanoparticles have been investigated against methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA) infections [22]. This may be due to the reasons that (i) ZnO nanoparticles interact more effectively with microbial cells because of their large surface area to volume ratio [22], (ii) destruction of microbial cell wall by the reactive oxygen species produced by ZnO [22] (iii) release of zinc (Zn2+) ions to disrupt biological functions of bacteria [22], and (iv) the lipid bilayer of the bacterial cell membrane can interact with ZnO nanoparticles and this contact may disrupt the membrane, allowing the leakage of internal components to trigger the cell death [23].

The recent key findings involved with ZnO/GQDs for the disinfection of different microbes is compared in Table S1 the supporting information. In a recent study, ZnO/GQDs with protoparmeliopsis muralis extract was used to disinfect MRSA and vancomycin-resistant Enterococcus faecalis (VRE) [24]. GQDs were particularly obtained from Pinto beans via a hydrothermal process. In fact, knowing that the production of GQDs depends on the supply of Pinto beans may limit its capacity to scale. A wide range of microorganisms, including E. coli, P. aeruginosa, B. cereus, and S. aureus were successfully disinfected by ZnO-nanoflakes@GQDs and ZnO-nanorods@GQDs [25]. In another study, E. coli was disinfected by a chemically synthesized ZnO/GQDs with polyethyleneimine (PEI) [26].

These recent findings clearly reveal that there are no detailed studies in the literature to disinfect MRSA isolated from clinical wound specimens using brewery spent grain (BSG) -derived GQDs with ZnO nanoparticles. BSG waste is made of cellulose, hemicellulose, non-cellulosic polysaccharides, and lignin, which are plentiful carbon sources [27]. The current study proposes the utilization of this carbon rich BSG as a raw material for the synthesis of GQDs, as an alternate strategy to reduce waste. For this, reduced graphene oxide (rGO) is initially produced from BSG waste (BrGO) using a low-cost catalytic oxidation process [28] and is then transformed into GQDs using the oxidative cleavage method. The disinfection mechanism for MRSA is elucidated for the first time with DFT calculations. The combined experimental findings of this study, backed by DFT calculations, unambiguously demonstrate that the highest level of antibacterial activity can be achieved by the synergistic activity of GQDs and ZnO nanoparticles.

The conversion of biomass and agricultural waste to carbonaceous nanomaterials is a crucial step towards the circular economy. Waste-derived carbonaceous nanomaterials have gained much attraction today because of their wide usage in biomedicine (antibacterial, antitumour, and bioimaging) [29]. Harnessing the potential of waste-derived carbonaceous materials (e.g. 2D materials, quantum dots) is not only promoting sustainable resource utilization but also providing innovative solutions to improve human health. Waste-derived carbon has characteristics equivalent to chemically produced carbon. Though originating from separate sources and experiencing various processing procedures, both kinds of carbon have identical structural and chemical properties. This equivalency highlights the possibility of waste-derived carbon as a feasible and sustainable option in several situations where chemically processed carbon has traditionally been used.

BSG was collected from Browar Jan Olbracht of Lodz, Poland. All the chemicals were of analytical grade and were used as received without further purification. DMEM high glucose, FBS, penicillin-streptomycin solution, trypsin, and DMSO were obtained from HiMedia. 3-(4,5- dimethylthiazol-2-yl)−2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide salt was purchased from Thermo Fisher Scientific. All other chemicals were procured from Sigma Aldrich.

A well-established catalytic oxidation process was used to produce BrGO [28]. At first BSG was washed several times with double distilled water, dried, and crushed into a fine powder. After that, 0.5 g of the BSG powder was mixed with 0.1 g of ferrocene and this mixture was calcined at 300 °C for 45 min in a muffle furnace.

The carbonization temperature and time were chosen based on the chemical composition and properties of the biomass. Cellulose, hemicellulose, and lignin are the major organic components of BSG [28]. These compounds are tightly packed and intertwined in the plant cell walls, which makes them more difficult to break down and carbonize. Additionally, BSG typically has a higher moisture content than other biomass (e.g. coconut coir), which can further slowdown the carbonization process. Decreasing the carbonization temperature was of special importance because it would further strengthen this method's environmental merits without lowering the material's quality. Therefore, in this study, the carbonization procedure was carried out at 300 °C for 45 min.

GQDs were produced by the oxidative cleavage approach using the few-layer BrGO flakes obtained from BSG [30]. BrGO (5 g) was suspended in 0.05 M H2SO4 (10 mL) and kept in an ice water bath for 4 h. Next, 50 wt% KMnO4 was added to the mixture, which was then stirred at room temperature for 1 h. BrGO can be exfoliated under acidic conditions. After being heated to 45 °C, the mixture was stirred for 1 h. Then, 5 mL of double distilled water was gently added to the solution, and the stirring was continued for another 30 min at 90 °C. This was followed by the dropwise addition of 10 mL of double distilled water with a small amount of H2O2 to complete the reaction. The pH of the orange-brown colored solution was adjusted to 8.0 using sodium hydroxide (0.05 M of NaOH) in an ice water bath followed by 1 h ultrasonication at 100 W. After obtaining a black flocculent deposit, pH was further adjusted to 4 by adding 1 M hydrochloric acid (HCl) The final reaction mixture was centrifuged for 5 min at 5000 rpm. GQDs were produced by collecting the supernatant [31].

ZnO nanoparticles were produced using a hydrothermal approach because it is scalable, affordable, eco-friendly, and high purity, as well as fostering the formation of nanoparticles with a limited size distribution and shape. For the synthesis, 0.13 M zinc nitrate (Zn (NO3)2.6 H2O) was prepared in 200 mL of double distilled water. To this, 1.25 mL of glycerol was added which serves as the capping agent under constant stirring. This was gradually mixed with 5.59 g sodium carbonate (Na2CO3) and then the mixture was heated to boil. After that, 1.25 mL of glycerol was again added, and the reaction mixture was stirred for 15 min. After cooling down, the solution was transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and maintained at 80 ° C for 12 h. The resulting precipitates were finally filtered, repeatedly washed with double distilled water and ethanol, and then dried in a hot air oven for 24 h. The as-obtained ZnO nanoparticles were then calcined for 2 h at 350 °C in a muffle furnace.

ZnO/GQDs composite was prepared by a similar procedure described in Sections 2.3, and 2.4. 25 mL of solution A was prepared after obtaining the pH 4 of the BrGO reaction mixture by treating it with NaOH and HCl. 200 mL of solution B was prepared by adding 0.5 g Zn (NO3)2.6 H2O, 0.61 mL of glycerol, and 0.196 g of Na2CO3 in double distilled water. Then solution B was added gradually to solution A and the whole mixture was stirred at room temperature for 10 min. To foster the formation of ZnO/GQDs, the reaction mixture was transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and maintained at 80 ° C for 12 h. The resulting precipitates were finally filtered, washed (with double distilled water and ethanol), dried, and calcined at 350 °C for 2 h.

MIC was used to determine the susceptibility of bacterial strains to the as-prepared nanomaterials [32]. The MIC test determines the lowest concentration of an antimicrobial agent that can inhibit the growth of a particular bacterial or fungal strain. In other words, the MIC represents the minimum concentration of a drug needed to prevent visible growth of the microorganism but does not necessarily kill the organism. The MIC test was typically performed by measuring the turbidity of a bacterial culture in the presence of increasing concentrations of the drug. A stock solution of ZnO, rGO, GQDs, and ZnO/GQDs was prepared using sterile tryptone soy broth (TSB) at a concentration of 200 μg/mL. Six dilutions with concentrations of 100, 50, 25, 12.5, 6.25, and 3.12 μg/mL were then prepared. Six tubes with various concentrations of the solutions were used in this experiment. All the solutions contained 0.1 mL of an Escherichia coli (E. coli) and Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) culture (3 × 108 CFU/mL). In each tube, there is a mixture of 2.5 mL of sample solution, 2.5 mL of TSB, and 0.1 mL of bacterial suspension. In a reference tube, the bacterial solutions were taken without the sample. All the tubes were incubated at 37 °C for 18 h. After the incubation period, the turbidity of the bacterial cultures in the presence of nanomaterials was measured using UV–vis spectroscopy at a wavelength of 600 nm.

MRSA strains were inoculated onto agar plates, and the bacteria were allowed to grow until they covered the entire surface of the agar (confluency). This approach was taken to assess the impact of the synthesized nanomaterials on inhibiting bacterial growth and spread, evaluating their effectiveness against the proliferation of MRSA. Sidra Medicine, Doha, Qatar, is where original cultures were gathered. The antimicrobial activity of nanomaterial was evaluated against clinically important bacteria (Staphylococcus aureus ATCC 25923, Escherichia coli ATCC 35218, MRSA ATCC43300, and Clinical MRSA isolated from wound sample) by standard colony forming unit (CFU) method. All procedures were performed in a sterilized condition inside the BSL-2 cabinet and the growth media used for the antimicrobial test were sheep blood agar. Nanomaterials (ZnO and ZnO/GQDs) were dissolved in sterile water and sonicated in a water bath for 2 h. After sonication, nanomaterials were diluted with saline to obtain three different concentrations such as 500, 750, and 1000 ug/mL respectively. All three bacterial cultures were grown on sheep blood agar plates, and the culture was diluted approximately to 104 CFU/mL concentration in PBS. Different concentrations of ZnO and ZnO/GQDs were added into sterile 1X PBS. Blank PBS without any nanomaterials served as the negative control. Each PBS was inoculated with 1 mL of bacterial culture and placed in a shaking incubator at 200 rpm for 3 h. A sterile PBS was used for serial dilution from each experiment including controls. 100 μL of a 104 dilution was plated on a fresh sheep blood agar plate using a sterile inoculation loop. The plates were inverted and incubated at 37 °C for 24 h. All the assays were carried out in duplicates. The colony-forming units were calculated to obtain the mean and standard deviation as follows.

CFU × mL = no . of colonies x dilution factor / volume of culture plate
(1)

Well-diffusion and drop assay [33] methods were also used to assess the antibacterial activity of as-synthesized nanomaterials against MRSA. In a nutshell, three distinct concentrations, including 200 μg/mL, 400 μg/mL, and 600 μg/mL, were utilized for the well diffusion test, while the drop assay method was carried out with concentrations of 100 μg/mL, 50 μg/mL, and 25 μg/mL. 5 mg vancomycin disc was used as the standard antibiotic. The agar plates were incubated overnight at 37 °C. After a 24 h incubation period, the zone of inhibition (in mm) was determined.

The murine pre-adipocyte cell line (3T3) was procured from the National Centre for Cell Sciences (NCCS), Pune, India, and maintained in a CO2 incubator at 37 °C, 5% CO2 and 95% relative humidity. The growth medium used is DMEM high glucose supplemented with 10% FBS and 1% penicillin-streptomycin solution. Cells were grown up to 70–80% confluency and then trypsinized for carrying out further experiments. The mitochondrial integrity and activity are interpreted as a measure of cell viability.

MTT assay was used to examine the cytotoxicity of the nanomaterials. The samples (GQDs, ZnO, and ZnO/GQDs) were prepared at different concentrations (0.1, 2.5, 5, 10, 25, 50 µg/mL) using DMEM serum-free media. Cells were grown in a T75 flask until they reached up to 70–80% confluency, then trypsinized and seeded in 96 well plates (5000 cells per well) for 24 h in a CO2 incubator at 37 °C, 5% CO2 and 95% relative humidity. The cells were then treated with the sample solutions and incubated for another 24 h at 37 °C. After this, samples were removed and then 10 µl of 5 mg/mL MTT reagent was added to all the wells and subsequently incubated for 4 h. After the incubation period, 190 μl of DMSO was added to all the wells to dissolve the formazan crystals and kept at 37 °C for 20 min. The absorbance was measured at 570 nm using a microplate reader. Cells treated with DMEM medium alone and those treated with Triton-X 100 were considered as negative and positive control respectively. The cell viability percentage was calculated as follows:
(2)

DFT calculations were employed to provide information about the molecular structures of GQDs and to elucidate the antimicrobial mechanisms. It assisted in figuring out how the carbon atoms, functional groups, and defects in the GQDs structure are arranged. Several functionals in combination with different basis sets, as implemented in Gaussian packaged have been compared (Table S2 in Supporting Information). Stationary points are confirmed by vibrational analysis (3n-6 real vibrations). Visualization of theoretical results is carried out using the Gaussview program.

Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FT-IR Nicolet 6700 spectrophotometer, Thermo Scientific, USA) was used to validate the existence of functional groups in the nanomaterial. The analysis was carried out in ATR (Attenuated Total Reflection) mode. At the interface between the ATR crystal and the specimen, an IR typically enters the crystal at a 45° angle (relative to the crystal surface) and is totally reflected.

The crystalline features were investigated by X-ray diffraction (XRD, Empyrean Panalytical Diffractometer). The diffraction patterns were generated using a symmetrical theta-theta scan in the Bragg-Brentano geometry and a cobalt anode tube producing irradiation of length = 1.79 Å.

Field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM, Zeiss, model LEO 400, Germany) was used to analyse the morphology. The specimen was mounted on a metal stub using an adhesive carbon disc that improves conductivity. The specimen surface was covered with a conductive substance to stop charge buildup. The metal was applied in a precise way using a sputter coater. The coating must be thick enough to inhibit charging (usually around 10 nm), yet not so thick as to cover the specimen's surface features.
Raman spectroscopy (WITec confocal alpha 300RSA + microscope (WITec GmbH, Ulm, Germany)) with an excitation wavelength of 532 and 633 nm was used to examine the structural features.

The bonding interactions, defects, and oxidation state of elements were analysed by an X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Scientific K-Alpha Plus). For the survey and high-resolution spectra, correspondingly, spectra were acquired in continuous scanner energy modes with pass energies of 200 and 50 eV and a step size of 0.4 and 0.1 eV. The spot size was 400 m, and the X-ray source was monochromated Al K. Thermo Scientific Avantage V5.980 software was utilized for data collection and processing. A Thermo Scientific Vacuum Transfer Module (VTM) was used to transfer samples from the glovebox to the spectrometer without exposing them to the outside air.

For the photoluminescence (PL) analysis, the spectra were acquired with a Perkin Elmer Lambda 900 spectrophotometer. Corrected (for source intensity) steady-state emission spectra were recorded with a HORIBA Jobin-Yvon Fluorolog-3 FL3–211 spectrometer equipped with a 450 W xenon arc lamp, double-grating excitation, and single-grating emission monochromators (2.1 nm/mm dispersion; 1200 grooves/mm), and a Hamamatsu R928 photomultiplier tube.

To conduct the high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) analysis, a Talos F200S G2 Transmission Electron Microscope operating at 300 kV was utilized. At first, the specimens were rinsed to remove any remaining fixative. After the samples were prepared by sonicating them in an isopropanol solution, a small amount was put on a copper grid coated in carbon. Prior to the examination, the samples were allowed to completely dry in the air.

The Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS apparatus from Malvern, UK was used for the Zetapotential measurements. It has a He–Ne laser that operates at 633 nm. Sea buckthorn oleosomes were produced in aqueous solutions of the nanomaterials. The tests were executed in an automatic duration mode in a disposable cell, with a minimum of 50 runs at a temperature of 25 °C. The Zetasizer program was used to examine the gathered data.

The examination of probable Atomic force microscopy (AFM) imaging of the nanoparticles was carried out under ambient conditions using a multimode atomic force microscope equipped with a Nanoscope V controller (Bruker Corporation). Measurements were made in tapping mode and the size of the images was 10 × 10 and 20 × 20 μm2. Commercial silicon probes type OTESPA (MicroMasch), with tetrahedral shape of the AFM tips with nominal tip radius <7 nm, cantilever spring constant of 26 N/m, and resonant frequency of 300 kHz were used. Image acquisition was performed with use of Nanoscope 7.3 software and further image processing was done using Nanoscope Analysis 1.9 (Bruker Corporation). The nanoparticles size distribution was calculated using SPIP 6.4 image processing software (Image Metrology A/S).

FT-IR: The functional groups of the samples were identified by FT-IR ( Fig. 1). In the FT-IR spectrum of BrGO, the peaks at 1050 cm−1 and 1223 cm−1 are responsible for the C-O (epoxy) and C-OH stretching vibrations, respectively [28]. The peak at 1678 cm−1 typically corresponds to the CC stretching vibration of sp 2 carbon atoms in the graphitic structure [34]. This indicates the presence of the CC stretching peak and the restoration of the conjugated π-bonding in the graphene lattice. The peak at 2845 cm−1 indicates the presence of aliphatic C-H stretching vibrations [35].

A distinctive band at 881 cm−1 in the FT-IR spectrum of ZnO nanoparticles indicates the Zn-O stretching vibration [36]. The peak at 1087 cm−1 corresponds to the symmetric stretching vibration of the Zn-O bonds [37]. Zn-H stretching vibration is noticed at 2331 cm-1. Furthermore, the peak at 1400 cm−1 in the ZnO nanoparticles represents the C-N stretching vibration of impurities present on the nanoparticle surface [38]. The peak observed at 1530 cm−1 is ascribed to the stretching vibration of O-H bonds in water molecules adsorbed on the nanoparticle surface [39].

FTIR spectra of GQDs exhibit a strong peak at 1649 cm−1, which represents the stretching vibration of the CO bond in carbonyl functional groups [40]. The peak at 1094 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of C-H bond [41]. The stretching vibration of OH groups in GQDs appeared at 3318 cm−1. This may be attributed to the presence of oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of GQDs.

The stretching vibrations of ZnO are evident from the peak at 624 cm−1 [42]. ZnO typically exhibits vibrational modes in the IR range, such as lattice vibrations, Zn-O stretching, or Zn-O bending modes [43]. The peak could indicate a phonon mode resulting from the coupling or hybridization of vibrational modes between ZnO and GQDs [42]. Such interactions can arise from physical adsorption, charge transfer, or other surface effects between the two components. Once ZnO is reacted with GQDs, the C = C and C = O peaks become narrower because of the strong interaction between GQDs and ZnO. The absorption band at 1106 cm−1 further validates the existence of C-O in ZnO/GQDs [44]. Such functional groups can serve as interaction sites. This peak could indicate a phonon mode resulting from the coupling or hybridization of vibrational modes between ZnO and GQDs. Such interactions could arise from physical adsorption, charge transfer, or other surface effects between the two components.

XRD: The crystalline features of BrGO, ZnO, GQDs and ZnO/GQDs were determined using XRD. The diffraction peak of BrGO at 2θ = 21.5° is indexed to the (002) crystal plane of rGO [45] (Figure S1 (a)). The interlayer distance (d) of BrGO is calculated as 2.20 nm using Bragg’s equation [46]. The small interlayer distance is attributed to a small amount of surplus oxygen-bearing functional groups or structural defects. Graphite typically has a ‘d’ spacing of 0.3354 nm, whereas turbostratic carbon has a ‘d’ spacing in the range of 3.38–3.41 nm [47]. The presence of intercalated oxygen groups or water molecules, which leads to an increase in the spacing between the layers, is responsible for the appearance of the shoulder peak at 2θ = 16.1° for BrGO [45]. The broad peak observed at 25.28° for the GQDs may be attributed to the π-π stacking (Figure S1 (a)). The (002) plane of GQDs (peak at 2θ = 25.28°) appears relatively small, suggesting that the GQDs possess a thinner morphology compared to the BrGO flakes. The absence of a peak in the vicinity of 2θ = 5–20° clearly demonstrates the distinction between BrGO and the GQDs [48].

For ZnO nanoparticles, there are five main characteristic peaks present at 2θ = 31.8° (002), 34.2° (101), 36.5° (110), 47.74° (103), and 56.59° (202), which can be indexed as a hexagonal Wurtzite phase (JCPDS card no. 36–1451) [49]. The spectra of ZnO also show some minor diffraction peaks at 2θ = 68.2° (200), 71.3° (112) and 74.1° (201) plane [50,51].

There is a decrease in the intensity of ZnO peaks in the ZnO/GQDs nanocomposite occurred. This can be due to the incorporation of GQDs that cause crystal defects and are likely related to the lattice disorder and strain in the ZnO lattice [52].

There is an additional peak at 2θ = 25.81° in the ZnO/GQDs composite due to the epitaxial growth and formation of the (100) plane [51] (Figure S1 (b)). Another additional peak at 2θ = 23.84° may be associated with the diffraction from GQDs present in the ZnO/GQDs composite. The average crystallite sizes of ZnO and ZnO/GQDs are calculated as 31 nm and 27 nm, respectively using Scherrer’s formula [53]. The decrease in the crystallite size of the ZnO/GQDs could be attributed to the nucleation effect of GQDs [9]. The presence of GQDs can provide favorable sites for ZnO nucleation, leading to the formation of smaller ZnO crystallites. As a result, the average crystallite size in the composite may be smaller than that of pure ZnO.

The micro strains were calculated as 2.27 με for ZnO and 4.98 με for ZnO/GQDs using the following equation,
(3)
ε = β ( hkl ) cos ( θ ) / 4 sin ( θ )
Where ε is the microstrain, β is the full width at half maximum (FWHM) in radians, and θ is the diffraction angle.
GQDs may generate lattice defects, alter crystal formation, or affect ZnO/GQDs interaction, increasing microstrain in the composite material.

FE-SEM: The FE-SEM images of BrGO, ( Fig. 2(a) and (b)) showed a sheet-like structure, which is due to the graphene layers being stacked on top of each other with a high aspect ratio. ZnO nanoparticles exhibited a nearly spherical-shaped morphology with uniform aggregation and displayed well-defined grain boundaries (Fig. 2(c) and (d)). The electro-negative character of oxygen present in ZnO and the sp 2 hybridization of graphene enhance their interaction. As a result, ZnO and GQDs can develop a solid bond [54].

EDS: The chemical composition of BrGO (Figure S2 in the supporting information) was confirmed by the EDS. This indicates that the sample is composed primarily of carbon (80% by weight) and oxygen (18% by weight) with some trace elements such as Mg (0.13%), Al (0.06%), Si (0.18%), P (0.35%), S (0.10%), K (0.11%), Ca (0.14%), and Zn (0.17%). This is because the BSG contains a variety of organic and inorganic materials, including these trace elements [4]. These trace elements were carried over into the final product during the synthesis processes. These elements are typically present in very low concentrations in the waste material, and their presence in the BrGO is likely due to their incorporation into the graphene lattice during synthesis. Trace elements can significantly impact the properties of BrGO, including its antimicrobial activity, electrical conductivity, mechanical strength etc. For example, magnesium has been shown to enhance the mechanical properties of graphene, while zinc can improve its electrical conductivity and antimicrobial activities [55]. Therefore, the presence of these trace elements in BrGO may be beneficial for certain applications such as antimicrobial, catalysis, and environmental and can contribute to the material’s unique properties [56].

Raman: To analyze the degree of graphitization, the existence of defects or functional groups, and for gazing at possible modifications in the vibrational modes and the bonding between ZnO and GQDs, Raman spectroscopy was used. Fig. 3 displays the Raman spectra of BrGO, GQDs, ZnO, and ZnO/GQDs. The existence of lattice disorders is indicated by the D band at 1348 cm-1 (Fig. 3(a)). The G band is identified at 1587 cm−1 [57] and the ID/IG ratio for BrGO is estimated to be 0.95. The multi-layered nature of the as-prepared BrGO is confirmed since the magnitude of the 2D band is lower in comparison to that of the D band [58]. By the group theory for ZnO, the optical phonons A1 + 2B1 + E1 + 2E2 are predicted by a hexagonal Wurtzite structure that falls into the space groups P63mc or C46v [59]. Accordingly, the E2 (TO) -E2 (LO) mode vibrational excitation is noticed at 328 cm−1 and the A1 (TO) mode is observed at 380 cm−1 [59]. The E1 (TO) mode is identified at 412 cm−1, while the E2 mode is noticed at 439 cm−1. When E1 (LO) is activated at 610 cm−1, the vibration mode becomes apparent [59]. The surface optical (SO) phonon modes are responsible for a peak at 650 cm−1 [60] (Fig. 3(b)).

In the ZnO/GQDs nanocomposite, a blue shift of the E2 H peak to 437 cm−1 suggests a reduction in the particle size and strain within the crystal structure (Fig. 3(c)). This is in good agreement with the microstrain results obtained from XRD. The peak at 386 cm−1 in ZnO nanoparticles is attributed to the E1 L mode or the longitudinal optical phonon mode [61]. The E2 H peak intensity at 639 cm−1 in ZnO is also reduced in ZnO/GQDs composite due to the charge transfer processes altering the local electronic environment or modifying the vibrational modes associated with the E2 H peak [62]. In the ZnO/GQDs nanocomposite, the decrease in Raman peak intensity is attributed to smaller crystallite sizes, which can result in broader peaks with reduced intensity. This is also in good agreement with XRD results (crystallite size of ZnO is calculated as 31 nm and ZnO/GQDs composite is calculated as 27 nm). The typical GQDs peaks are witnessed at 1330 and 1589 cm−1, correspondingly, the D-band (disordered sp 2 carbon) and other G-band (ordered graphite). The Raman maps (Fig. 3(d), (e), and (f)) shed light on the degree of graphitization, graphitic nature, and any structural alterations brought occurred by interactions with other materials. A comparison of Raman spectra analysed at different excitation wavelengths is represented in Figure S3 in the Supporting Information. The results showed that there is no difference in the Raman peak position at 532 nm and 633 nm excitations. This is due to efficient PL quenching by the surrounding molecules or defects [63].

XPS: The survey spectrum of ZnO, BrGO, and ZnO/GQDs is demonstrated in Fig. 4(a). A significant peak at 285.0 eV is ascribed to the C1s and O1s binding energy of BrGO carbon with sp
2 hybridization [64]. The peak at 1021.4 eV in the spectrum of ZnO nanoparticles is associated with the binding energy of the O 1 s [65]. In the survey spectrum of the ZnO/GQDs, a distinct peak is observed at binding energies characteristic of the Zn 2p, typically around 1021.5 eV for Zn 2p3/2 and 1043.29 eV for Zn 2p1/2 transitions [65]. The O 1 s peak is observed around 530.0 eV typically representing the oxygen components for ZnO (Fig. 4(a)).

The C1s spectra of BrGO display a prominent peak at 285.0 eV, which is characteristic of graphitic carbon [66] (Fig. 4(b)). The O1s peak observed at 532.9 eV in the O1s spectrum of BrGO indicates the presence of various chemical states or oxygen-containing functional groups, including O-H, C-O-C, and CO [67]. This is in good agreement with the FT-IR results.

Brewery waste may contain organic compounds, such as nucleotides or phospholipids, that inherently contain phosphorus. During the reduction process, these organic compounds can contribute phosphorus atoms and P-C bonds, resulting in the appearance of a P 2p peak at 133.7 eV in the spectrum of BrGO [68] (Fig. 4(c)).

The C 1 s spectrum of ZnO (Fig. 4(e)) displays a peak at 285.06 eV, suggesting the presence of carbon. This may be due to the reason that the surface of the ZnO nanoparticles may absorb carbon-containing molecules or species from the surrounding atmosphere, such as hydrocarbons. The O1s peak at 529.9 eV is associated with oxygen atoms bonded to zinc atoms in the ZnO lattice (Fig. 4(f)). In the case of ZnO nanoparticles, the 2p peak at 1021.4 eV is typically associated with 2p3/2 transitions [69] (Fig. 4(g)).

The peak at 532.1 eV corresponds to the O 1 s transition associated with the oxygen bonding in the material, including Zn-O bonds [70]. The peak at 530.0 eV is due to the presence of Zn-OH. This suggests that the composite contains oxygen atoms that are chemically bonded to the carbon atoms within the GQDs. The peak that appeared at 1021.5 eV and 1044 eV are associated with the 2p3/2 and 2p1/2 electronic configuration of the Zn atoms respectively [65] (Fig. 4(i)).

PL: The PL emission spectrum of GQDs with peaks at 420 nm and 540 nm indicates that the GQDs emit fluorescence in both blue and green regions of the electromagnetic spectrum [71] (Figure S4 (a)). The emission peak at 420 nm is associated with blue light emission [72]. This emission is typically associated with the recombination of electron-hole pairs within the GQDs. The exact mechanism for the blue emission depends on various factors such as the size, structure, and surface functionalization of the GQDs. It is possible that the blue emission arises from quantum confinement effects within the GQDs [73]. The emission peak at 540 nm indicates green light emission in GQDs [74]. It can be attributed to different phenomena, including defect states or surface functionalization [75].

The simultaneous presence of blue and green emission peaks in a single PL spectrum suggests the coexistence of GQDs with different sizes with diverse surface states or functional groups. Each size or surface state can contribute to a specific emission peak. The variation in size or surface functionalization can lead to different energy levels and electronic transitions within the GQDs, resulting in distinct emission wavelengths [76]. This is supported by the TEM size histogram since the average particle size from the size histogram obtained is 23.77 nm.

There is a reduction in PL intensities of GQDs upon the incorporation of ZnO (Figure S4 (b)), suggesting that the presence of ZnO may cause surface passivation of the GQDs. Surface passivation involves the modification of the GQDs surface to reduce the number of surface defects or trap states [77]. On the other hand, ZnO may form surface complexes with functional groups on the GQDs surface. These surface complexes can modify the electronic structure and energy levels, leading to changes in the radiative properties and lower PL intensities [78]. ZnO incorporation can also introduce additional charge-trapping centers within the GQDs. These trapping centers can capture excited electrons or holes, reducing the radiative recombination efficiency and resulting in lower PL intensities. When ZnO nanoparticles are incorporated into GQDs, there is a shift in the peak from 540 nm to 500 nm. This is because the surface characteristics and charge distribution of the material change when ZnO is added to GQDs. These modifications to the surface groups and charge behavior affect the motion of the electrons within the GQDs, affecting their light-emitting characteristics and producing apparent shifts in the emission characteristics observed in the PL spectra.

HR-TEM: The morphology and microstructure of the nanomaterials were investigated by the HR-TEM analysis. The multi-layered morphology of as produced BrGO sample is confirmed by the HR-TEM ( Fig. 5(a)). The d spacing of GQDs was calculated as 0.293 nm (Fig. 5(b)). From the size distribution histogram of GQDs, the average particle size was calculated as 23 nm (Fig. 5(c)). The nearly spherical-shaped morphology of ZnO nanoparticles was confirmed (Fig. 5(d)). As shown in Fig. 5(e), the spacing distance of these three samples is 0.25 nm, which corresponds to (0 0 2) planes of the wurtzite ZnO. From the size distribution histogram of ZnO nanoparticles, the average particle size of the ZnO nanoparticle obtained is 27 nm (Fig. 5(f)).

Zeta potential: The stability of the as-prepared nanomaterials (ZnO, GQDs, and ZnO/GQDs) in the aqueous solution was assessed, and the surface charge was determined using the zeta potential analysis. Fig. 6 shows that all the materials examined (GQDs, ZnO, and ZnO/GQDs) are negatively charged (for ZnO (−34 mV), GQDs (−27 mV), and ZnO/GQDs (−38 mV)). The negative zeta potential values indicate an increased stability of nanoparticle colloidal dispersions. Functional groups like carboxyl (COOH), and hydroxyl (OH) are frequently present on the surface of GQDs. These functional groups can ionize in aqueous solution and add to a net negative charge on the nanomaterial surface. ZnO nanoparticles with a negative zeta potential typically have more OH groups on their surface. This surface OH groups have the potential to ionize at neutral pH, leaving the particle surface net negatively charged [79]. In comparison to pure ZnO, the ZnO/GQDs exhibited superior dispersion in water since it possess more negative values. This suggests that the inclusion of GQDs has a notable impact on enhancing the stability of ZnO nanoparticles in the solution. The increased stability and dispersion of ZnO/GQDs facilitate better accessibility and contact with bacterial cells. This leads to an overall increase in antimicrobial activity, which is further enhanced by the inherent antibacterial features of ZnO and its possible interactions with GQDs.

AFM: To examine the surface topography of the nanomaterials, AFM analysis was employed (Figure S5). This result is based on measurements of height collected at numerous sites across the topographical image range. AFM results reveal a relatively uniform dispersion of ZnO nanoparticles with an average particle size of around 49 nm (Figure S5 (a). The AFM image of ZnO/GQDs show bright spots that suggest the existence of nanosized GQDs [80] (Figure S5 (c)). The AFM results of the ZnO/GQDs composite provide information about the surface characteristics and distribution of GQDs within the ZnO matrix.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of GQDs formation from BSG
ce:section.ce:para: BSG consists of various components according to the origin, age, and date of production. Cellulose and hemicellulose are the major components of BSG [14]. Due to the intricate structure of the multicomponent carbon source, the mechanism of BrGO, and GQDs formation from BSG is also complex. It includes a wide range of chemical reactions that may take place simultaneously or subsequently. The schematic of the BrGO and GQDs formation mechanism is shown in Fig. 7. In the catalytic oxidation process, the vaporization of water and the evaporation of highly volatile substances present in it take place. Moreover, carbon molecules undergo partial decomposition and are subsequently released, contributing to an increase in the porosity of the solid material.

The volatile components may include a wide range of condensable and non-condensable molecules. This could contain CO,CO2, more compact molecules like furan-ring products and anhydrosugars, as well as smaller molecular species like aldehydes, alcohols, and acids [28]. Ferrocene concurrently exceeds its boiling point at temperatures above 249 °C, allowing for efficient access to interact with the carbon component as it becomes increasingly porous. This makes it easier to catalyze secondary reactions that involve both solid and vaporized carbon components. At this point, ferrocene has the power to introduce an electron into the other carbon molecules to start various modifications. A one-electron oxidation of ferrocene can reversibly produce ferrocenium ions, which play a role in both hole and electron catalysis [28]. As expected for BrGO, the combination of these processes produces graphene-like structures characterized by sp
2 hybridization. FTIR and XRD results support this conclusion.

BrGO formed in this manner is subsequently subjected to oxidative cleavage, leading to the formation of GQDs. As a result of the oxidative cleavage of BrGO, which is made possible by the presence of KMnO4 and H2SO4, smaller pieces of rGO are formed. Through a series of pH adjustments and ultrasonication, GQDs are formed. The first pH adjustment with NaOH and the following pH adjustment with HCl both serve multiple purposes. The pH alteration has the first impact on the surface charge of the BrGO fragments. When the alkalinity rises to pH 8, functional groups on the surface of the BrGO fragments, including -COOH, -OH, and epoxy groups, among others, can deprotonate in this alkaline environment.

The BrGO surface develops negatively charged sites because of this deprotonation, giving the fragments a net negative surface charge [30]. Due to this, electrostatic repulsion forces form between the BrGO pieces, preventing them from adhering together and increasing their dispersion in the solution. As a result, the larger BrGO fragments cannot come together to form new, and larger structures. On the other hand, when the environment gets more acidic, i.e., at pH 4, the functional groups on the BrGO surface protonate, flipping the charge distribution [32]. BrGO pieces can approach and recombine because the positive charges generated by protonation offset the repellent forces. Smaller GQDs are eventually formed because of this reassembly process, which is distinguished by their nanoscale size and distinctive optical features. To achieve the appropriate nanoscale structure and characteristics, this regulated assembly is essential.

Ultrasonication has two key purposes when it comes to GQDs creation. First, by creating mechanical forces and cavitation, it helps to break up larger BrGO fragments into smaller ones [32]. To produce nanoscale quantum dots, this fragmentation procedure is necessary. Moreover, ultrasonication encourages the even distribution of fragments throughout the solution, thereby limiting their aggregation, a critical factor in achieving homogeneous GQD production.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of GQDs formation by computational studies
ce:section.ce:para: To prepare a valid model reflecting the appearance of zero-dimensional GQDs, it was necessary to start by preparing small graphene oxide (GO) leaves. Initially, GO was described as a structure with a random distribution of flat aromatic regions with non-oxidised benzene rings, with undulating regions rich in hydroxyl and epoxy functional groups, together with carboxyl groups at the edges of the graphene wafers [81]. The refinement was made by noting that functional groups may create clusters and hydroxyl pairs exhibit anti-position in the basal plane [82]. However, studies of graphene surfaces at the atomic level using Molecular Dynamics (MD) simulations have neglected the interactions of functional groups with the aqueous external environment [82]. Further research on GO layers has confirmed its chemical activity in the water with interactions affecting the final GO wafer structure and acquiring an average negative charge of the order of 10 mC/m2 on its surface [82]. Following the latter and considering the dimensions of the GQD, a GQD realistic model composed of 20 graphene cells and 59 carbon atoms using Gassview v6.1 has been constructed.

The chemical functional groups were grafted to the surface in a ratio of 2 hydroxyl groups per 1 epoxide group, placed on both sides of the graphene plane [82]. Hydroxyl groups were systematically placed in pairs and clustered on the GQD layer. The functionalization rate of prepared surfaces was around 18 functional groups on 59 carbon atoms, O/C=18/59 ∼ 30% which is a typical value for GO flakes ( Fig. 8). The resulting structures overlapped with structures proposed in literature [82] and compared. Bearing in mind that, GQD properties strongly depend on the number, type, concentration, and distribution of functional groups on the surfaces, 12 different GQD models that differ in the level of clustering of functional groups on the surface, and one pure graphene model for comparison were built. Then the models were subjected to a series of optimization and final refinement by using ωB97x-D/def2TZVP [83] level of theory with Gaussin16 with D3 dispersion correction by Grimme. A detailed table with different theory level comparisons is provided in Table S1 in the supporting information, where it was compared how much the geometry of structure w1 and its frequencies are distorted because of optimization at different levels of theory compared to the geometry obtained after long dynamics in water [82].

How much distortion occurred in the C-carbon chain alone, in the carbon chain with heavy oxygen atoms CO, and in the whole COH structure was examined. To avoid wrapping the graphene surfaces and to better recreate the reaction medium, knowing that the water environment influences the FT-IR spectra, a continuous model of the PCM water solvent in the calculations was used. Along with the optimization, the FTIR and Raman spectra were calculated and compared them with measured spectra for GQD flakes from BSG a brewery (Figure S6 and Figure S7 in the supporting information). Clusters of functional groups on the GQD surface ensure the reactivity of the material and influence the charge distribution on the surface, which in turn can also influence the interactions between adjacent GQD flakes. To get insight into charge distribution the molecular ESP (Electrostatic Potential) distribution with CHELPG (Charges from Electrostatic Potentials using a Grid) charges, (Fig. 8) were calculated. The average difference between optimized structures and their initial geometry is measured using RMS (Root Mean Square) values (Table S2).

Oxygen-bearing groups have a greater tendency to cluster on the GQD surface than to randomly cover it, as observed previously. This results in a smaller surface area being corrugated, which is more beneficial energetically. Moreover, the clustered functional groups on the surface interact with each other via internal hydrogen-bond stabilization, which provides further energy compensation.

From the 12 GQD leaves (Fig. 8), based on the FT-IR and Raman spectra obtained and compared with the experimental results, one structure w4 whose spectra best reflected the experiment was selected. The chosen structure is characterized by a high level of clustering of functional groups on both sides of the surface, particularly at both edges of the GQD surface. Clustering at edges was confirmed to have an antimicrobial impact against the bacterial species. Moreover, this molecule has a high ESP charge polarization, with stable charge separation. This charge polarization in the structure promotes the generation of free electrons and holes and supports efficient electron transport. Oxygen-bearing groups have a greater tendency to cluster on the GQD surface than to randomly cover it [84]. This results in a smaller surface area being corrugated, which is more beneficial energetically. Moreover, the clustered functional groups on the surface interact with each other via internal hydrogen-bond stabilization, which provides further energy compensation.

To assess the safety, biocompatibility, and suitability for different applications, BrGO, ZnO, GQDs, and ZnO/GQDs composites must undergo cytotoxicity testing. These tests are necessary to ensure the safety of people and the environment, to comply with regulations, and to comprehend any potential health hazards linked to these products. A notable and extensively used cell model for evaluating cytotoxicity is 3T3 mouse embryonic fibroblasts (MEFs). The murine pre-adipocyte cell line (3T3) was used in this study to analyse the cytotoxicity, and the MTT assay was used to quantitatively measure cell viability. The results showed a viability percentage of more than 75% for all samples with a concentration of 0.1, 2.5, 5, 10, 25, and 50 µg/mL thereby showing the non-cytotoxic nature ( Fig. 9). Biocompatibility is important because it allows these materials to safely interact with living systems, which is essential for their successful integration into biomedical systems or devices. When interacting with biological tissues or organisms, biocompatible materials lessen the possibility of negative reactions, inflammation, or rejection, opening the door for safer and more efficient biomedical applications.

Minimum inhibition concentration (MIC) of ZnO, BrGO, GQDs, and ZnO/GQDs nanomaterials against the E. coli (Gram-negative) and S. aureus (Gram-positive) were assessed by a two-fold serial dilutions approach with 6 diluted forms of 100, 50, 25, 12.5, 6.25, and 3.12 μg/mL samples and chloramphenicol was used as a standard antibiotic. Results show that all four samples have a MIC of 3.12 μg/for both E. coli and S. aureus. From Fig. 10 (a) and (b), it is noticed that the percentage of inhibition increases with respect to the concentration of all samples.

Although Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria have different cell wall architectures, ZnO/GQDs still demonstrate almost similar effectiveness against both types of bacteria, since there are slight variations in the killing mechanisms. The thick peptidoglycan (20–80 nm) layer of Gram-positive bacteria cell wall protects them from the effects of antimicrobial agents [85]. The surface proteins, teichoic and lipoteichoic acids, and other polymers are retained by this thick encasing of peptidoglycan [86]. Since amino acid and carbohydrate repeating units can be found in the peptidoglycan layer, ZnO/GQDs can bind with amino and carboxylic acid groups to hinder biological activities.
The cell membrane breaks more easily in Gram-negative bacteria because their peptidoglycan layer is thinner than that of Gram-positive bacteria [87].

They are less vulnerable to some antibacterial agents because of the outer membrane, which serves as a barrier. However, the outer membrane of Gram-negative bacteria may become more permeable and vulnerable to the subsequent attack of ZnO/GQDs on the cell membrane because of the reactive species produced by ZnO/GQDs. Because of the surface functional groups like -COOH and -OH groups, which can participate in redox reactions that result in the generation of reactive species like hydroxyl radicals, GQDs can display oxidative characteristics even in the absence of photoexcitation [88].
Gram-negative bacteria have a complexly organized cell wall. A lipopolysaccharide-containing exterior membrane and a thin peptidoglycan layer are present [89].

The concentration-dependent effect further enhances their antimicrobial activity against both types of bacteria, making them promising candidates for broad-spectrum antimicrobial applications. Even though, the peptidoglycan layer does influence the susceptibility of bacteria to antimicrobial agents, the main target of ZnO/GQDs' reactive species is the cell membrane. The reactive species can disrupt the cell membrane's integrity and lead to cell lysis and death. This critical step occurs beyond the peptidoglycan layer and is common to both Gram-negative and Gram-positive bacteria. This non-specific mechanism is not dependent on targeting specific components of the cell wall. Instead, reactive species can cause damage to various cellular components, including proteins, lipids, and DNA, leading to cell death [90]. As a result, ZnO/GQDs can effectively inhibit the growth of both Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria.

The antimicrobial efficiency was also tested against MRSA by the drop and well diffusion methods. ATCC strains, and clinical strains isolated from wound specimens were introduced into plates and permitted to develop until confluency was reached. The plates were made with wells and drops that contained different concentrations of BrGO, ZnO, and ZnO/GQDs, with the standard antibiotic vancomycin. Following a 24 h incubation period, zones of inhibition were detected for the drop diffusion method ( Fig. 11). No zones of inhibition were obtained for the well-diffusion method for all the samples for both the MRSA strains. This could be attributed to the reason that the drop diffusion method typically involves applying a small volume of antimicrobial agent onto the surface of the agar, creating a localized high concentration that gradually diffuses into the surrounding medium.

As a result, a concentration gradient is produced, which may result in a zone of inhibition that is more precise and effective. Another reason is that the antibacterial agent can interact with the bacteria more successfully using the drop diffusion approach because it can diffuse more gradually and under control. Moreover, when using the well-diffusion method, a larger amount of antimicrobial agent in the well may saturate the surrounding agar, reducing the diffusion's effectiveness and the inhibition zone. The risk of agar saturation is lower with the drop diffusion method since it utilizes fewer antimicrobial agents.

There is no discernible zone of inhibition found in BrGO samples for ATCC MRSA strain (Fig. 11 (a)). This may be due to the poor dispersibility of BrGO in the solvents. The poor dispersion of the material may cause reduced contact with bacteria, hindered diffusion, and limited surface area due to the aggregation of BrGO sheets. To address this issue, the BrGO can be surface-functionalized. It is possible to considerably improve the solubility of BrGO in a wide range of solvents, including ethanol, dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), and tetrahydrofuran (THF), by adding the right functional groups, such as hydroxyl, carboxyl, or amine, to the surface of the material [91]. The addition of these functional groups encourages interactions with the solvents via hydrogen bonds or van der Waals forces, which improves BrGO's dispersion and solubility [92,93].

ZnO nanoparticles alone didn’t show any activity against MRSA (Fig. 11 (b)). But when it is combined with GQDs, the zone of inhibition is increased. This may be a result of the interaction between the two materials, which could raise the formation of reactive species and boost their total bactericidal properties. Another reason can be the dispersion of ZnO nanoparticles in the bacterial growth medium was enhanced by GQDs. For ZnO/GQDs composite, the maximum zone diameters (at 100 µg/mL) are observed as 9 mm for ATCC MRSA strain (Fig. 11 (c)). For MRSA clinical isolates from wound specimens, ZnO/GQDs exhibited a zone diameter of 8 mm for the concentration of 100 µg/mL (Fig. 11 (d)). This implies that BSG-derived GQDs could possibly play a significant role in battling antibiotic-resistant bacteria, supporting global efforts to address the problem of antimicrobial resistance.

The percentage of inhibitions was calculated as 80%, 76%, and 70% for ZnO/GQDs against ATCC strains and 61%,71%, and 85% against clinical isolates for the concentrations of 100 µg/mL, 50 µg/mL, and 25 µg/mL respectively. It was reported that the inhibition of MRSA was only partially achieved when using ZnO nanoparticles with particle sizes exceeding 100 nm [25] . FE-SEM analysis of ZnO nanoparticles revealed a broad particle size distribution ranging from 100 to 200 nm. This wide size range might be responsible for the incomplete inhibition of MRSA observed in this experiment.

The survival rate of clinical MRSA, S. aureus, and E. coli strains were also calculated with different concentrations of samples and the results were displayed as colony-forming units (CFU/mL) (Fig. 11 (e), and (f)). Dose-dependent measurement of survival showed inhibition of the bacteria by ZnO and ZnO/GQDs in the range of 500 −1000 μg after exposure for all three microbial species. S. aureus and E. coli showed inhibition when exposed to ZnO and ZnO/GQDs within the dosage range, as measured by the survival rates, which were dose dependent. Similar results were shown in the case of clinical MRSA, where significant inhibition was seen after exposure. However, it is noted that there is an increase in the number of viable count of bacteria at 1000 μg. While overly high concentrations of ZnO and ZnO/GQDs may kill bacteria less severely, allowing some to survive, lower levels of ZnO and ZnO/GQDs demonstrate antibacterial characteristics. This is ascribed to the MRSA can develop defenses against antibiotics. Fighting these resilient bacterial strains can be difficult because MRSA has the genetic flexibility to trigger defense mechanisms that negate the effects of antibacterial agents [94]. These bacterial strains may activate defense mechanisms, such as efflux pumps or biofilm formation, at increasing concentrations to survive and adapt to the challenging environment the materials generate.

MRSA could use biofilm formation as a defense mechanism. Bacterial communities arranged in a structured manner and encased in an extracellular matrix that produces itself provide resistance against antimicrobial agents [95]. Another potential factor is the aggregation of nanoparticles may reduce their effective dispersion, lowering their contact with bacteria and, potentially, their antibacterial activity.

The antimicrobial efficiencies of ZnO/GQDs are extremely helpful for many applications, particularly in the biomedical and healthcare industries. Thus, these nanomaterials can be used as potent antimicrobial agents to treat bacterial infections, particularly those brought on by strains that are resistant to common antibiotics. To stop bacterial colonization and infection, they can be added to medical implants, wound dressings, or coatings. This lowers the risk of implant-related infections and problems following surgery [96]. Additionally, because they work well at low concentrations, there may be less chance that they may be poisonous or have negative effects on the host cells, making them safer for medicinal applications. Furthermore, a minimal quantity of nanomaterials would be needed to produce the desired therapeutic effects and that results in cost-effectiveness in medical applications.

Some modifications can be taken into consideration to increase the effectiveness of ZnO/GQDs nanocomposites for commercial applications such as surface functionalization for enhancing biocompatibility and stability. The biocompatibility and stability of these materials can be further enhanced by adding more functional groups to their surfaces by combining GQDs with bioinert materials such as polyethylene glycol (PEG) [97]. Multiple bonding locations for antibiotic conjugation and enhanced cell absorption capacity could be provided by GQDs when it is combined with PEG and GQDs/PEGs could increase cell viability by 95% [98] . The effectiveness of ZnO/GQDs can be improved by considering the modifications.

The growing interest in studying the antimicrobial effects of graphene-based materials resulted in several hypotheses of mechanisms of action including nano-knives, oxidative stress, lipid extraction, protein disruption, and wrapping or trapping [6]. Undeniably, the shape, size, number of layers, and distribution of the functional groups on the BrGO surface influence its antimicrobial activity, nevertheless, the mechanism of action itself remains poorly understood, especially since there are many inconsistent conclusions that exist in the literature with opposite statements [1]. Coarse-grained MD simulations confirm that smaller GO fragments penetrate bacterial phospholipid bilayers more easily compared to larger GOs, which remain glued to the surface. All-atom MD simulations were used to indicate that smaller GO layers may have a stronger antimicrobial effect [2] , although a different team of researchers ended up with a completely different conclusion [51]. Moreover the frayed shape and sharp edges with the smooth top side of GOs were confirmed to decrease the survival rates of S. aureus.

Sharp edges enable GOs to penetrate the S. aureus membrane more easily, with a lower energy barrier, as confirmed by MM/MD (molecular mechanics/molecular dynamics) simulations. As has also been demonstrated, GO surface modifications increase its surface energy and thus its antimicrobial properties against S. aureus. The initial focus of antimicrobial treatment often involves targeting the outer membrane of the system under investigation. Both theoretical and experimental evidence has demonstrated that GO induces the disruption of E. coli's phospholipid layer through a potent dispersion interaction between GO and the cell membrane [99]. Similarly, the hydrophilic head of phospholipids, with a phosphate group, can interact with the surface of ZnO/GQDs. The connection between the charged or polar functional groups on the composite surface and the phosphate groups of the phospholipids may entail electrostatic attractions or hydrogen bonding. When the hydrophilic head area is attached to ZnO/GQDs, a stable interface between the composite and the phospholipids can occur.

Moreover, the MM/MD simulations proved that the phospholipid layer may be penetrated by small graphene nanosheets due to hydrophobic interactions, while bigger GOs suffocate the microbe by lying on the membrane surface and preventing contact with the environment [6]. This raises the question of whether similar properties of GO structures can be used against MRSA. As the outer membrane of MRSA being the target of antimicrobial effects of GOs has the same structure and composition as an S. aureus membrane, we can consider the same mechanisms of action. Importantly, however, studies of the antimicrobial impact of GO to date have focused on studies of a simplified outer membrane model that completely ignored the existence of a thick peptidoglycan layer. Here, we would like to point out that the outer wall of S. aureus is a cross-linked hydrogen-like cell envelope, that is approximately 20–40 nm thick layer ( Fig. 12).

The presence of peptidoglycan, an important part of bacterial cell walls, has been shown to have a considerable impact on the antibacterial modes of action of GQDs. Peptidoglycan offers skeletal support to bacterial cells and is essential for their continued functioning [100]. GQDs can interact with the peptidoglycan layer of bacteria, resulting in several antimicrobial actions such as GQDs can harm the bacterial cell wall by disrupting the peptidoglycan layer. This disruption compromises the cell's strength, rendering it more sensitive to mechanical stress and osmotic pressure [101]. As a result, the bacterium might suffer cell lysis or release of cellular parts, ultimately leading to cell death.

Furthermore, the combination of GQDs and peptidoglycan might cause oxidative stress in bacterial cell [101]. GQDs are naturally oxidative, and when they encounter the peptidoglycan layer, they can produce reactive species [102]. These reactive species, such as hydroxyl radicals, can damage parts of cells including proteins, lipids, and DNA, disrupting key functions in cells, and eventually causing the breakdown of bacterial cells [103].

Moreover, in the presence of peptidoglycan, the unique surface features of GQDs, such as their high charge polarization and stable charge separation, can improve their antimicrobial activities [104]. The charged structure of peptidoglycan can promote electrostatic interactions with GQDs, resulting in higher binding affinity and improved bacterial cell localization [105]. This interaction may stimulate the formation of free radicals and reactive species near the peptidoglycan layer, enhancing the antimicrobial actions (Fig. 12).

In general, the existence of peptidoglycan in bacterial cells has been shown to have a considerable impact on the antimicrobial modes of action of GQDs. GQDs' high antibacterial effect against bacteria is attributed to cell wall rupture, development of oxidative stress, increased internalization, and interaction with intracellular components. These findings emphasize GQDs' potential as promising medicines for fighting bacterial infections, utilizing the interactions between GQDs and peptidoglycan to deliver antimicrobial effects.

The primary objective of this study was to explore the synergistic interaction between BSG-derived GQDs and ZnO nanoparticles and its impact on MRSA inhibition. Several unique interactions between the two materials, ZnO and GQDs result in improved bactericidal characteristics. ZnO and GQDs work well together to foster the production of reactive species. Since reactive species have been demonstrated to harm bacterial cell membranes, proteins, and DNA, resulting in bacterial mortality, their presence can have a substantial impact on bactericidal activity. In addition, the incorporation of GQDs improves ZnO nanoparticle dispersion in the bacterial growth medium. The better dispersion of these nanomaterials increases their interaction with the MRSA cells. As a result, more bacteria are exposed to antibacterial traits, which further contributes to the observed increase in the zone of inhibition.

Furthermore, BSG-derived GQDs may also act as carriers or stabilizers for ZnO nanoparticles, preventing their agglomeration and facilitating their interaction with MRSA. The surface functional groups on the GQDs may encourage ZnO nanoparticles to adhere to bacterial cell surfaces more strongly, strengthening their antibacterial actions. The focus of this study was on the combined effect of BSG-derived GQDs and ZnO nanoparticles. Future studies may delve deeper into the individual contribution of pure GQDs to the antibacterial properties to provide a comprehensive understanding of their role in enhancing the overall antibacterial activity of the composite material.

From the results, we propose that BSG, which is generally discarded after the brewing process, can be used again to make products with added value and substantial market potential. ZnO/GQDs nanocomposite presents a promising alternative to standard antibiotics for the treatment of MDR microorganisms. These nanoparticles might be used in a variety of fields where antimicrobial capabilities are sought to increase durability and safety, including coatings, textiles, and food packaging. Environmental sustainability is reflected in the use of BSG as a source for GQDs and the production of antibacterial nanoparticles. It offers an environmentally friendly substitute for traditional antibacterial compounds while reducing waste and promoting resource efficiency. The potential for BSG-derived GQDs to be a successful and long-lasting treatment for antibiotic-resistant bacteria gives them their futuristic commercial use. A circular economy model can be formed by turning brewery waste grain into antimicrobial nanoparticles, providing a variety of business opportunities, and supporting environmental and global health activities.

The existence of MRSA within MDR organisms necessitates intensified endeavours in the advancement of novel medications and the implementation of advanced preventative measures. MRSA continues to be considered a very significant threat to public health due to its remarkable adaptability and capacity to propagate resistance. Therefore, addressing the infectiousness of fully virulent MRSA strains remains a critical priority. Other alternative therapeutic methods for MRSA are being developed, but there is no timeline or assurance that they will be successful in a clinical study. Thus, in this study, we explore the potential of harnessing the combined effect of ZnO and GQDs for the efficient eradication of MRSA. For this, GQDs were successfully synthesized from BrGO via catalytic oxidation followed by oxidative cleavage, and uniformly distributed ZnO/GQDs were produced via hydrothermal technique. The sheet-like morphology of BrGO was confirmed by FESEM analysis. The presence of blue and green emissions in the PL spectrum confirms the presence of GQDs with different sizes. The primary peaks at 285.0 Ev and 530 eV are attributed to the C1s and O1s binding energy of BrGO, according to the XPS spectra; for ZnO/GQDs samples, the main peaks occur at 285.0 eV and 290 eV, respectively. The size distribution histogram showed that the average particle size for GQDs is 27.33 nm.

The results displayed substantial antibacterial activity against E. coli, S. aureus, and MRSA (for both ATCC strain and clinical isolates from wound specimens). The tandem interactions of GQDs and ZnO enhanced the generation of free radicals, cell wall breakage, the development of oxidative stress, and contact with intracellular components. The findings imply that diligent fabrication of functional nanocomposites could culminate in a significant improvement in antibacterial effectiveness. All nanomaterials (rGO, GQDs, ZnO, and ZnO/GQDs composites) showed a remarkably high viability percentage of more than 75%. This result is quite significant since it shows that these materials may be useful in a variety of biomedical and biotechnological applications where biocompatibility is a key factor. In conclusion, this study can be considered a preliminary investigation into the antimicrobial activity of ZnO/GQDs nanoparticles against MRSA. It could be used in a variety of medical and healthcare contexts, including wound dressings, coatings for medical equipment, packaging, and surface treatments to prevent the spread of infections in hospitals.
